threats.title: Locking Outside Active Lockdrop Period Zeroes Stored Remainder
threats.hypothesis_description: In _lock, the local variable remainder is initialized to 0. The block that computes the new remainder only executes during an active lockdrop. Outside the lockdrop period, remainder stays 0. At the end of _lock, lockedToken.remainder is unconditionally overwritten with this local variable, destroying any previously accumulated remainder from a lockdrop.
threats.investigation_rationale: The unconditional assignment lockedToken.remainder = remainder at line 343 overwrites the stored value. When the lockdrop block (lines 324-335) is skipped, remainder = 0, destroying any prior value. This is a state corruption bug.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256 remainder; (defaults to 0) at line 315; conditional NFT calculation at lines 324-335; unconditional lockedToken.remainder = remainder at line 343

preconditions: User accumulated a non-zero remainder from a previous lockdrop lock
User locks additional tokens outside an active lockdrop period

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Local variable defaults to 0; conditional assignment only during lockdrop; unconditional storage write
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: lockedToken.remainder silently zeroed for any lock outside lockdrop
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should preserve existing remainder when lockdrop not active, or scope the assignment inside the lockdrop conditional

analysis_questions: What is the typical remainder value in economic terms?
Can users avoid this by not locking outside lockdrop?
Does this interact with the phantom remainder bug (TECH-H005)?
validation_requirements: Lock during lockdrop to create remainder
Lock additional tokens after lockdrop ends
Verify remainder is now 0

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User locks 150 tokens during lockdrop (nftCost=100). Gets 1 NFT, remainder=50. Lockdrop ends. User locks 60 more tokens. Outside lockdrop, no NFT calc runs. lockedToken.remainder = 0 (local default). User loses 50 tokens of NFT progress permanently.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of partial NFT credit (remainder value); up to nftCost-1 tokens worth per user per token
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any user who locks outside lockdrop with existing remainder
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Permanent loss — remainder cannot be recovered

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for locking outside lockdrop period with pre-existing remainder

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing remainder before/after lock outside lockdrop

threats.title: lockOnBehalf Can Overwrite Recipient's Lock Duration Preference
threats.hypothesis_description: In _lock, if the lock recipient's playerSettings.lockDuration is 0 (never set by the user), _lockDuration falls back to lockdrop.minLockDuration. At the end of _lock, playerSettings[_lockRecipient].lockDuration is unconditionally set to this value. An attacker can use lockOnBehalf to force-set a victim's lock duration to the lockdrop minimum before the victim sets their own preference.
threats.investigation_rationale: The combination of the fallback logic and the unconditional write to playerSettings means any caller of lockOnBehalf can influence the recipient's future lock behavior. This is a sovereignty violation where the recipient's preferences are overwritten without consent.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (_lockDuration == 0) { _lockDuration = lockdrop.minLockDuration; } ... playerSettings[_lockRecipient].lockDuration = _lockDuration; — unconditional overwrite

preconditions: Recipient has never called setLockDuration (lockDuration == 0)
Recipient is a registered main account
Token is active and configured

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unconditional write to recipient's playerSettings from lockOnBehalf
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Attacker sets victim's lockDuration to lockdrop.minLockDuration before victim configures their own preference
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should only write playerSettings for msg.sender, not for _lockRecipient in lockOnBehalf

analysis_questions: Can the victim override this by calling setLockDuration afterward?
Does this interact with the unlockTime griefing (TECH-H002)?
What is the practical impact on victim's future locks?
validation_requirements: Call lockOnBehalf for a new user who hasn't set lockDuration
Verify victim's playerSettings.lockDuration is now set to lockdrop.minLockDuration
Verify victim must explicitly call setLockDuration to change it

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: New user registers. Attacker calls lockOnBehalf(token, 0, victim) before victim sets lockDuration. Victim's lockDuration is set to lockdrop.minLockDuration (e.g., 30 days). Victim later locks large amount expecting to use a longer duration but doesn't realize their duration was already set.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Victim may lock with unintended short duration, affecting reward calculations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: New users who haven't yet configured lock duration
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Victim can call setLockDuration to fix

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for lockOnBehalf's effect on recipient's playerSettings

threats.title: ERC20 unlock may silently fail or revert for non-standard tokens due to missing SafeERC20 handling
threats.hypothesis_description: The contract uses IERC20.transfer/transferFrom directly without SafeERC20. Tokens that return no boolean (or return false instead of reverting) can cause reverts (ABI decode) or silent failure, resulting in incorrect accounting: lockedToken.quantity is decreased but user may not receive tokens (if token returns false but doesn’t revert).
threats.investigation_rationale: Project documentation explicitly expects support for weird ERC20s. Direct calls are brittle and can break core user withdrawals, leading to trapped funds or accounting desync. This is practical and frequently exploited/triggered by real tokens.
deployment_assumptions: At least one configured token is non-standard (no return value, returns false, or has non-compliant behavior)
Admin intends to support such tokens per docs
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence provided that only strictly OZ-compliant ERC20s will be configured; docs explicitly state weird ERC20 support is expected.

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC20(_tokenContract).transferFrom(...) and IERC20(_tokenContract).transfer(...) used without SafeERC20

preconditions: A non-standard ERC20 is configured and active for locking
Users lock and later unlock that token
Token behavior includes missing return value / returning false / atypical revert behavior

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No SafeERC20 wrapper; return values not handled
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Unlock could decrement internal balance then fail to transfer (if token returns false without revert) or revert due to ABI decoding (no return)
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No balance-delta accounting; no require(success) pattern for low-level call

analysis_questions: Do any configured tokens in production return false (instead of revert) on transfer failures?
Is the IERC20 interface imported from OZ (expects bool), causing decode revert on no-return tokens?
Could silent failures lead to lockedToken.quantity decreasing without user receiving tokens?
validation_requirements: Test with a mock ERC20 that returns false on transfer
Test with a mock ERC20 that returns no value (USDT-style) and confirm behavior under solidity 0.8.25
Check whether any upstream contracts rely on lock/unlock success assumptions

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If a token returns false but does not revert, attacker triggers unlock() to reduce their recorded locked quantity while receiving nothing, potentially causing later system inconsistencies (e.g., rewards/points accounting) and trapping funds. More commonly, users get stuck because transfers revert for non-standard tokens.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Funds frozen for that token; possible accounting desync if transfers fail silently
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Medium (could be mitigated by migrating balances if supported)

threats.test_coverage_gap: No evidence of weird-ERC20 compatibility tests despite documented requirement

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Compare internal accounting vs ERC20 balance movements in traces

threats.title: USD price proposal cannot execute when APPROVE_THRESHOLD == 1 because proposeUSDPrice never calls _execUSDPriceUpdate() after auto-approval
threats.hypothesis_description: proposeUSDPrice increments approvalsCount and records the proposer approval, but does not call _execUSDPriceUpdate(). Therefore, even if APPROVE_THRESHOLD is set to 1, the proposal will not be executed until a separate approveUSDPrice call occurs, effectively making the minimum approvals 2 and breaking admin-configured governance semantics.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is an explicit logic defect affecting governance behavior. While not an attacker fund-drain, it can break operations if the admin intentionally sets threshold to 1 for emergency updates (or accidentally sets it).
deployment_assumptions: Admin sets APPROVE_THRESHOLD to 1 via setUSDThresholds
Protocol expects immediate execution on proposal creation when threshold is 1
threats.config_evidence: Thresholds are settable via setUSDThresholds(uint8,uint8) with no bound checks in LockManager.

proof_of_signal.pattern: proposeUSDPrice sets approvalsCount++ but does not call _execUSDPriceUpdate()

preconditions: APPROVE_THRESHOLD is configured as 1
A price feed operator calls proposeUSDPrice
No other operator calls approveUSDPrice

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Auto-approval not followed by threshold check/execution
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Operational emergency price updates may be blocked
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No immediate execution path in proposeUSDPrice

analysis_questions: Is APPROVE_THRESHOLD ever intended to be 1 in production or only for tests?
Do any deployment/config scripts set thresholds to 1?
Would an operator be incentivized to withhold the second approval to freeze prices?
validation_requirements: Unit test: set threshold to 1, propose, verify no update occurs until approveUSDPrice called
Check for any tooling that assumes 1 means immediate execution

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If threshold is set to 1 for operational reasons, any single operator can prevent updates by not sending the second approve tx; usdPrice remains stale, impacting rewards.
prerequisites: Threshold misconfiguration or emergency mode reliance

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Indirect: stale price feeds distort reward/weighting calculations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users relying on usdPrice-integrated flows
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: High (can restore threshold or have another operator approve)

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observe proposal remaining active with approvalsCount==1 and no state changes

threats.title: getLockedWeightedValue may revert or overflow with misconfigured decimals/large values, breaking downstream reward distribution if used on-chain
threats.hypothesis_description: getLockedWeightedValue computes deltaDecimal = 10**(18 - decimals). If decimals > 18, subtraction underflows and reverts. Additionally, (deltaDecimal * quantity * usdPrice) can overflow before division. If any critical state-changing function in other contracts calls getLockedWeightedValue internally (e.g., during reward distribution), this can halt protocol operations.
threats.investigation_rationale: Per constraint, view-function DoS only matters if it propagates into state-changing paths. Phase A indicates weighted value likely drives reward calculations in external systems. Need to confirm call graph usage and whether these reverts can block claims/period transitions.
deployment_assumptions: At least one token is configured with decimals > 18 OR users can reach extreme quantity/price product causing overflow
A state-changing function in another contract calls LockManager.getLockedWeightedValue() and propagates revert
threats.config_evidence: Docs state tokens with very high (>18) decimals may be supported; LockManager does not validate decimals <= 18.

proof_of_signal.pattern: deltaDecimal = 10 ** (18 - configuredTokens[token].decimals); multiplication before division may overflow

preconditions: Token configured with decimals > 18 OR extremely large quantity*usdPrice values
Some critical write-path depends on getLockedWeightedValue result (must be confirmed)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unchecked exponent and large intermediate multiplication
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Protocol-wide DoS if revert bubbles into state-changing reward logic elsewhere
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No decimals bounds validation; no mulDiv/checked scaling to avoid overflow

analysis_questions: Which contracts call getLockedWeightedValue on-chain (AccountManager/ClaimManager/etc.)?
Are those calls in critical state-changing paths (claim, distribute, period rollover)?
What are realistic maximum quantities and usdPrice values on Blast for configured tokens?
validation_requirements: Search codebase for internal/external calls to getLockedWeightedValue from non-view functions
Fuzz decimals around 18 and large quantities to test revert/overflow
If used in a write path, add invariant tests ensuring it never reverts for configured tokens

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If a high-decimal token is configured and active, any reward distribution function that queries weighted value can revert, halting claims/period updates for all users until reconfiguration.
prerequisites: Misconfiguration or deliberate addition of a token with decimals > 18
Integration uses this view in state-changing logic

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Indirect: claim/reward operations blocked; potential protocol halt
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Potentially all users if global operations depend on weighted values
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Medium (remove/deactivate token or fix decimals, but requires admin action)

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing boundary tests for decimals>18 and large-value arithmetic

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Triggered whenever weighted value is queried in write paths
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor reverts in downstream contracts calling this view

threats.title: setLockDuration Bypass Allows Immediate Withdrawal of Time-Locked Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: The setLockDuration function validates new unlock time using `block.timestamp + _duration >= old unlockTime` but computes the actual new unlockTime as `lastLockTime + _duration`. As time passes since lastLockTime, the gap between block.timestamp and lastLockTime widens, allowing users to pass the validation check with a small duration that sets unlockTime to a past timestamp, enabling immediate withdrawal.
threats.investigation_rationale: This directly violates the author-stated invariant 'Users must not be able to reduce their previously set lockup times' and enables theft of lockdrop NFT rewards by unlocking before the committed duration expires.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Validation uses `uint32(block.timestamp) + uint32(_duration) < unlockTime` but assignment uses `lastLockTime + uint32(_duration)` — these diverge as time passes

preconditions: User has tokens locked with quantity > 0
Sufficient time has elapsed since lastLockTime (past midpoint of lock duration)
Contract is not paused

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Check: block.timestamp + _duration >= unlockTime. Assignment: unlockTime = lastLockTime + _duration. When block.timestamp >> lastLockTime, small _duration satisfies check but produces past unlockTime.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: User locks 1000 ETH for 365 days during lockdrop, receives NFT rewards. At day 300, calls setLockDuration(66 days): check 300+66=366>=365 passes, but new unlockTime=0+66=day 66 (234 days in the past). Calls unlock immediately.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker retains all lockdrop NFT rewards (worth the full locked amount / nftCost NFTs) while recovering 100% of locked principal after only a fraction of the committed duration.

economic_questions: What is the exact time threshold at which a user can begin reducing their unlock time?
What is the value of NFT rewards relative to the opportunity cost of the shortened lock?
Can this be combined with the phantom remainder carry-over to amplify NFT farming?
validation_requirements: Prove lock-then-early-unlock sequence with specific timestamps in a PoC
Calculate NFT reward value for typical lock amounts against nftCost configurations
Verify that any user past the midpoint of their lock can unlock immediately

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Any amount of lockable tokens (scales with desired NFT count)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: All lockdrop NFT rewards for the locked amount, with near-zero effective lock commitment
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Effectively infinite ROI on lock commitment (NFT rewards for free)
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Two transactions: setLockDuration + unlock (can be same block)

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: None — uses user's own locked tokens
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After passing the midpoint of any lock duration
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: No market impact; purely on-chain state manipulation

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Protocol and honest lockers who committed to full lock durations
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Lockdrop NFT dilution proportional to exploiter's share of total locks
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire TVL of locked tokens can be withdrawn early; all lockdrop NFT integrity compromised

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: lastLockTime vs block.timestamp divergence; MaxLockDuration from configStorage
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Prove that for lockDuration D locked at time T0, at time T0+D/2+1, the user can set duration to D/2 and unlock

threats.title: lockOnBehalf Enables Permanent Fund Freezing via Zero-Cost Unlock Timer Reset
threats.hypothesis_description: The _lock function unconditionally overwrites unlockTime to `block.timestamp + lockDuration` for the recipient. Any user can call lockOnBehalf with _quantity=0 (or 1 wei for ETH) for any victim, resetting their unlockTime to the full lock duration from the current timestamp. Repeated calls permanently prevent the victim from ever withdrawing their locked funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: Directly violates the author-stated invariant 'Funds must not get locked forever.' An attacker can grief any user at near-zero cost on Blast L2, permanently freezing potentially large amounts of locked value.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _lock unconditionally sets `lockedToken.unlockTime = uint32(block.timestamp) + uint32(_lockDuration)` without checking if new unlockTime > old unlockTime for existing locks, and lockOnBehalf permits any caller

preconditions: Victim has tokens locked with quantity > 0
Victim has a non-zero lockDuration set in playerSettings (or lockdrop.minLockDuration > 0)
Token used is configured and active
Contract is not paused

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: lockOnBehalf allows _quantity=0. For ERC20: allowance >= 0 always passes. transferFrom(attacker, this, 0) succeeds. unlockTime reset to now + victim's lockDuration.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker calls lockOnBehalf(tokenContract, 0, victim) just before victim's unlock time expires. Costs only gas (~0.0001 ETH on Blast). Victim's unlockTime extended by full lockDuration. Repeat indefinitely.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing attack — no direct profit but permanent denial of access to victim's locked funds. Could be weaponized for extortion or competitive sabotage.

economic_questions: Can 0-amount transferFrom succeed for all configured token types?
Does the victim's lockDuration fallback to lockdrop.minLockDuration if unset?
Can an attacker also overwrite a victim's playerSettings.lockDuration via this path?
validation_requirements: PoC: lock 100 ETH as victim, then as attacker call lockOnBehalf(address(0), 0, victim) with msg.value=0
Verify victim cannot unlock after the griefing call
Test with both ETH and ERC20 token paths

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: ~0.0001 ETH per griefing call on Blast L2
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: No direct profit — pure griefing. Victim impact: 100% of their locked balance frozen
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: N/A (griefing); could be monetized via extortion
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Single transaction per extension; repeat every lockDuration period

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Just before victim's unlock time expires (maximizes extension per call)
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: No market impact; targeted DoS against specific users

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Any user with locked tokens in the protocol
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire protocol TVL at risk of permanent freezing

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Verify 0-quantity lock path for both ETH and ERC20; check if _onBehalfOf=address(0) falls through to msg.sender
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace unlockTime changes through lockOnBehalf with 0 quantity

threats.title: Zero Lock Duration Enables Instant Lock/Unlock Cycle for Reward Gaming
threats.hypothesis_description: When lockdrop.minLockDuration is 0 (no lockdrop configured or stale lockdrop with minLockDuration=0) and a user has not set their own lockDuration, the effective _lockDuration defaults to 0. This sets unlockTime = block.timestamp, allowing immediate unlock within the same block. Since forceHarvest is called in both lock and unlock, the user can accumulate schnibble rewards without meaningful time commitment.
threats.investigation_rationale: The protocol's reward mechanism (schnibbles via forceHarvest) depends on the assumption that locked tokens remain committed for a duration. Zero-duration locks break this assumption and may allow reward extraction without real commitment.
deployment_assumptions: lockdrop.minLockDuration == 0 or no lockdrop configured
threats.config_evidence: Lockdrop configured via start-lockdrop.ts with minLockDuration parameter (not visible in script); if lockdrop expires or was never configured, lockdrop struct defaults to all zeros including minLockDuration=0

proof_of_signal.pattern: _lockDuration = playerSettings[_lockRecipient].lockDuration; if (_lockDuration == 0) { _lockDuration = lockdrop.minLockDuration; } — both can be 0, leading to unlockTime = block.timestamp

preconditions: No active lockdrop OR lockdrop.minLockDuration == 0
User has never called setLockDuration (playerSettings.lockDuration == 0)
User deploys contract to call lock() then unlock() in same transaction (sequential, not reentrant)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: unlockTime = block.timestamp + 0 = block.timestamp. Unlock check: unlockTime > block.timestamp is FALSE, so unlock allowed same block.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Deploy helper contract that calls lock(token, amount) then unlock(token, amount) sequentially. forceHarvest called in both lock and unlock with locked weighted value briefly non-zero.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Depends on forceHarvest implementation — if rewards calculated per-second, 0 seconds = 0 rewards. If any per-transaction or per-call reward component, may be extractable.

economic_questions: Does forceHarvest grant any rewards for zero-duration locks?
Can flash loans amplify this by locking large amounts briefly?
Does getLockedWeightedValue contribute to reward calculations even within a single block?
validation_requirements: Analyze AccountManager.forceHarvest implementation for time-independent reward components
Test lock+unlock in same block via helper contract
Verify lockdrop struct defaults when no lockdrop configured

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Depends on flash loan availability — potentially large amounts for brief period
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Unknown — depends entirely on forceHarvest reward mechanics

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Flash loan liquidity for the token being locked

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All legitimate stakers (reward dilution if applicable)
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional to attacker's flash loan size vs total locked value

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Lockdrop struct defaults; forceHarvest reward calculation model
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Determine if zero-duration lock yields any non-zero reward from forceHarvest

threats.title: USD Price Governance Vote Exclusivity Violation Enables Double-Counting and Stuck Proposals
threats.hypothesis_description: The disapproveUSDPrice function checks `approvals[msg.sender] == _usdProposalId` to prevent approvers from disapproving, but approveUSDPrice does NOT check `disapprovals[msg.sender] == _usdProposalId`. A price feed operator can disapprove first, then subsequently approve the same proposal, incrementing both counters. This breaks vote mutual exclusivity and can cause proposals to become permanently stuck.
threats.investigation_rationale: This weakens the multisig governance model for price updates. Under specific vote orderings, both approval and disapproval thresholds can be met simultaneously, causing _execUSDPriceUpdate to silently fail and the proposal to become stuck — no execution AND no deletion.

proof_of_signal.pattern: approveUSDPrice checks `approvals[msg.sender] == _usdProposalId` but never checks `disapprovals[msg.sender]`; disapproveUSDPrice sets disapprovals[msg.sender] but doesn't block later approval

preconditions: Active USD price proposal exists
At least one price feed operator can call both disapprove and approve
Vote ordering allows disapprove before approve for at least one operator

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: disapprovalsCount and approvalsCount can both be incremented by the same operator. In _execUSDPriceUpdate: `approvalsCount >= APPROVE_THRESHOLD && disapprovalsCount < DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD` — if both thresholds met, update silently fails.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Stuck proposal scenario: approval threshold reached but disapprovals also at threshold → _execUSDPriceUpdate does nothing, proposal stays (proposer != address(0)), blocking new proposals indefinitely until additional disapprovals force deletion.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Governance DoS — prevents price updates, causing stale prices that distort getLockedWeightedValue and downstream reward calculations.

economic_questions: What specific vote orderings lead to stuck proposals?
Can the stuck state be resolved without admin intervention?
What is the economic impact of stale USD prices on reward distribution?
validation_requirements: Simulate disapprove-then-approve sequence with 5 operators
Identify stuck proposal conditions for default thresholds (3/3)
Quantify reward distortion from stale prices

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: 0 (only gas costs for price feed operators)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: ~0.001 ETH gas per vote on Blast L2
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect — stale prices benefit some lockers over others depending on price direction
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Variable — depends on price staleness duration and price movement

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During volatile price movements when stale prices create maximum distortion
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Indirect — affects all users' weighted value calculations

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All protocol users affected by stale token prices
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional to token price movement during stuck period

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Model all vote orderings to find stuck states

threats.title: Fee-on-Transfer Token Accounting Insolvency Leading to Last-Withdrawer Loss
threats.hypothesis_description: The _lock function records `lockedToken.quantity += _quantity` using the requested transfer amount rather than the actual received amount. For fee-on-transfer tokens, the contract receives fewer tokens than recorded. Over time, total recorded quantities exceed the actual contract balance, causing later unlockers to be unable to withdraw their full recorded balance.
threats.investigation_rationale: The project documentation explicitly states the LockManager 'is expected to handle non-standard ERC20 behaviors, including fee-on-transfer.' The current implementation does NOT use a balance-before/after pattern, directly contradicting this stated requirement.
deployment_assumptions: A fee-on-transfer ERC20 token is configured as an active token
threats.config_evidence: Project documentation states fee-on-transfer support is expected. configure-token-test.ts configures USDB, WETH, ETH — whether any of these are fee-on-transfer on Blast depends on the specific token implementations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: token.transferFrom(_tokenOwner, address(this), _quantity) followed by lockedToken.quantity += _quantity — no balance delta check

preconditions: Admin configures a fee-on-transfer token (documentation says this should be supported)
Multiple users lock the fee-on-transfer token
Users begin unlocking their recorded quantities

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: lockedToken.quantity += _quantity records the pre-fee amount. Contract receives _quantity * (1 - feeRate). Deficit per lock = _quantity * feeRate. Cumulative deficit grows with each lock.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: With a 2% fee token: 50 users each lock 100 tokens. Contract records 5000 total, receives 4900. First 49 users unlock 100 each (4900 tokens). Last user has 100 recorded but 0 balance — permanent loss.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Early unlockers can withdraw full recorded amounts while the deficit accumulates on remaining users. An attacker who locks and quickly unlocks extracts more value than they deposited.

economic_questions: Are any Blast-native tokens (USDB, WETH) fee-on-transfer?
What is the rate of insolvency growth per user lock?
Can an attacker intentionally exploit the accounting gap by being an early unlocker?
validation_requirements: Check Blast USDB and WETH contract implementations for transfer fees
Model insolvency accumulation with N users and fee rate F
Verify that unlock transfers the full recorded quantity regardless of contract balance

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Early withdrawer profit = feeRate * total locked by others (accumulates over time)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Proportional to fee rate and number of subsequent depositors
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Gradual insolvency over multiple lock/unlock cycles

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After many users have locked the fee-on-transfer token
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Insolvency affects last N users proportional to accumulated fee deficit

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Last users to unlock the fee-on-transfer token
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Up to 100% loss of locked balance for the very last user
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Total fee deficit across all lock operations

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Blast USDB/WETH token semantics; any configured token fee rates
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify contract uses raw _quantity not actual received amount

threats.title: USD price proposal can become unremovable and unexecutable due to lack of any expiry/cancel path
threats.hypothesis_description: Once a USDUpdateProposal exists, there is no time-based expiry, no admin cancellation, and no proposer cancellation. If approvals never reach APPROVE_THRESHOLD and disapprovals never reach DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD, the proposal persists indefinitely and blocks new proposals (proposeUSDPrice reverts when proposer != address(0)). A single PriceFeed operator can grief by proposing and then the operator set can simply stop interacting, freezing all future USD updates.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a practical governance liveness failure with on-chain-only actions. Even without key compromise, operational outages or disagreement can stall price updates; an attacker with any PriceFeed role can force a stuck state without needing transaction ordering.

proof_of_signal.pattern: proposeUSDPrice/approve/disapprove require proposal state but no cancel/expiry function exists; proposeUSDPrice reverts if usdUpdateProposal.proposer != address(0)

preconditions: Attacker has any one of Role.PriceFeed_1..Role.PriceFeed_5
Attacker (or group) can ensure neither threshold is reached (e.g., by non-participation / coordination failure among operators)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Proposal lifecycle lacks a termination path besides reaching thresholds
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Admin cannot intervene unless BaseBlastManager provides an unshown override; not visible here
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time price feeds are intended to be updated

operational_questions: Is there an off-contract operational process to forcibly rotate price feed roles to recover liveness?
Does configStorage rotation of LockManager dependencies affect USD governance state?
Is usdUpdateProposal intended to be short-lived, and if so why is there no expiry?
validation_requirements: Confirm there is no external function in inheritance that deletes usdUpdateProposal
Simulate a proposal and verify new proposals are blocked indefinitely without thresholds

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Operator proposes a USD price update with any _contracts list
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Operator set does not reach either threshold (intentional non-participation)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: All future proposeUSDPrice calls revert due to ProposalInProgressError()
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Minimal if the attacker is the only active operator; otherwise requires exploiting operator inactivity

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: USD prices become stale forever; downstream reward/weighting logic can become inaccurate or halted
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium to high (depends on role rotation and whether role management is on-chain and responsive)
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Any subsystem relying on usdPrice freshness

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Long-lived usdUpdateProposal.proposer != 0x0 with no subsequent updates
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Reward calculations drifting or disputes over stale pricing

threats.title: USD Price Feed Distortion via Single Price Array Application
threats.hypothesis_description: The proposeUSDPrice function takes an array of token contracts but only a single USD price. When executed, this single price is applied to all tokens in the array, potentially causing massive mispricing if tokens with different values are bundled.
threats.investigation_rationale: If operators attempt to update WETH and USDC in the same proposal, one will be drastically mispriced, distorting the getLockedWeightedValue calculation which drives protocol rewards.

proof_of_signal.pattern: A single proposedPrice is applied in a loop to all tokenContract addresses in the proposal array

preconditions: Price feed operators propose a price update for multiple tokens with different actual USD values in a single transaction

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Operator error or malicious proposal bundles high-value and low-value tokens
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Users holding the artificially inflated token receive vastly disproportionate rewards

economic_questions: How does getLockedWeightedValue impact the downstream schnibbles emission?
Can operators be tricked into bundling tokens?
validation_requirements: Simulate a bundled proposal and measure the impact on getLockedWeightedValue

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Existing locked positions in the mispriced token
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: None for the user (relies on operator action)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Thousands of times normal reward rate (e.g., if USDC is priced at WETH value)
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Until a new price proposal corrects it

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Severe distortion of protocol economy and unfair reward distribution

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Holders of the correctly priced or undervalued tokens

threats.title: Phantom Remainder After Full Unlock Enables Discounted NFT Minting on Subsequent Lock
threats.hypothesis_description: When a user fully unlocks their tokens via unlock(), lockedToken.remainder is never cleared (only quantity is decremented). This leaves a phantom remainder referencing value the user already withdrew. On the next lockdrop, calling lock(token, smallAmount) causes _lock to compute quantity = smallAmount + phantom_remainder, granting NFTs based on the combined value. The user effectively receives NFTs at a discount proportional to the phantom remainder since those tokens are no longer locked.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code unconditionally preserves remainder across unlock operations. Since remainder is added to the new deposit amount during NFT calculation, any user who had remainder from a previous lockdrop and then fully unlocked can combine that phantom remainder with a smaller deposit to mint NFTs. This directly violates INV-ECON-002 (remainder must not exceed locked quantity) and represents measurable value extraction from the NFT distribution mechanism.

proof_of_signal.pattern: unlock() decrements lockedToken.quantity but never clears lockedToken.remainder; _lock() line 275 computes quantity = _quantity + lockedToken.remainder, including phantom remainder from already-withdrawn tokens in NFT calculation

preconditions: User locked tokens during a previous lockdrop and accumulated non-zero remainder
User fully unlocked all tokens (quantity=0, remainder>0)
A new lockdrop is active
User deposits additional tokens (even very small amount) to combine with phantom remainder

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _lock line 275: uint256 quantity = _quantity + lockedToken.remainder; line 283: numberNFTs = (quantity - remainder) / configuredToken.nftCost
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: After full unlock, remainder persists. On next lock during lockdrop, phantom remainder inflates NFT calculation. Example: user had remainder=50 after locking 150 tokens (nftCost=100), got 1 NFT. After full unlock, locks only 50 tokens in next lockdrop → quantity=50+50=100 → 1 NFT for 50 tokens instead of 100.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: User receives NFTs requiring nftCost tokens but only commits (nftCost - phantom_remainder) new tokens. Discount per NFT = phantom_remainder/nftCost * 100%.

economic_questions: What is the average remainder accumulated by users across different token configurations?
What is the economic value of each NFT minted via this discount mechanism?
Can the attack be repeated across multiple lockdrop cycles to compound the advantage?
validation_requirements: Confirm remainder persists after full unlock via unit test (lock → unlock all → check remainder)
Demonstrate NFT minting with phantom remainder by lock(token, small_amount) during next lockdrop
Calculate NFT value vs actual tokens committed to quantify discount

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: As low as 1 wei per NFT (plus phantom remainder from previous lock)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for lock+unlock+lock transactions (~0.001 ETH total)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Value of NFTs minted at discount; up to (nftCost-1)/nftCost discount per NFT
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Up to 99% discount on NFT cost depending on remainder size

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After first lockdrop ends and before second lockdrop starts (for unlock), then during second lockdrop (for discounted lock)
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: No market impact; attack operates entirely within protocol state

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Protocol NFT distribution mechanism; honest lockers who commit full nftCost
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Diluted NFT distribution; honest users get fewer proportional NFTs
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Depends on NFT economic value and number of users exploiting this pattern

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: nftCost per token (WETH: 1e16, USDB: 3e18 from test config); typical lock amounts
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm remainder is NOT cleared in unlock(); confirm _lock uses remainder in NFT calculation

threats.title: ETH Locked via lock() by Smart Contract Wallets Permanently Frozen Due to .transfer() Gas Limit
threats.hypothesis_description: The lock() function accepts ETH deposits from any caller including smart contract wallets (Gnosis Safe proxies, account abstraction wallets, etc.). However, unlock() returns ETH using payable(msg.sender).transfer(_quantity) which forwards only 2300 gas. Smart contract wallets whose receive/fallback functions require more than 2300 gas will fail to receive ETH, permanently locking their funds with no alternative withdrawal path.
threats.investigation_rationale: The lock() function has no restrictions on caller type and the project targets Blast where smart contract wallets may be common. The author-flagged risk area explicitly states 'Funds must not get locked forever.' The .transfer() pattern is a known anti-pattern that creates an irrecoverable fund-freeze for contract callers. There is no emergency withdrawal or alternative ETH claim mechanism in the contract.

proof_of_signal.pattern: payable(msg.sender).transfer(_quantity) in unlock uses 2300 gas stipend; no alternative ETH withdrawal method exists

preconditions: User interacts via a smart contract wallet (Gnosis Safe, AA wallet, etc.)
User locks ETH (tokenContract = address(0))
User's wallet contract requires >2300 gas in receive/fallback

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Not a manipulation but a permanent freeze: smart contract wallets that locked ETH cannot ever unlock it
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No profit for attacker; pure fund loss for affected users. Total at risk = all ETH locked by smart contract wallets.

economic_questions: What percentage of Blast users use smart contract wallets?
Is there any admin rescue mechanism for stuck ETH?
Does Blast's native ETH handling change the 2300 gas stipend behavior?
validation_requirements: Test unlock() from a Gnosis Safe proxy or other smart contract wallet
Verify no alternative ETH withdrawal path exists in the contract
Check if Blast network modifies .transfer() behavior

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N/A (not an attack; it's a fund freeze for legitimate users)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: N/A - this is permanent fund loss for affected users
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Permanent freeze once ETH is locked from qualifying wallet

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time a smart contract wallet user locks ETH
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: No market manipulation needed; purely a code incompatibility issue

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Any user with a smart contract wallet that locked ETH
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All ETH locked by smart contract wallet users

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Test with Gnosis Safe proxy (most common SC wallet on EVM chains)
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify .transfer() fails for common SC wallet implementations on Blast

threats.title: Gas Exhaustion DoS in Core Functions due to Unbounded Token Array
threats.hypothesis_description: The `configuredTokenContracts` array grows unboundedly with each new token configuration. Functions like `setLockDuration` and `getLockedWeightedValue` iterate over this entire array, leading to a permanent DoS if the array becomes too large.
threats.investigation_rationale: If the admin configures many tokens over the protocol's lifecycle (even if they are later marked inactive), core functionalities will eventually exceed the block gas limit and break permanently.
deployment_assumptions: Admin configures a large number of tokens over time
threats.config_evidence: Deployment scripts show multiple tokens being configured, but no mechanism exists to remove them from the array.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `for (uint256 i; i < configuredTokensLength; i++)` iterating over unbounded array

preconditions: Admin has configured a large number of tokens (e.g., > 100)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Unbounded iteration over a state array that only grows
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Once the array reaches a critical size

operational_questions: How many tokens are expected to be supported?
Is there a plan to deprecate and remove old tokens?
validation_requirements: Configure 200 mock tokens
Attempt to call `setLockDuration` and verify it reverts due to out-of-gas

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Admin routinely adds new tokens for various promotional events or lockdrops
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: The gas cost of iterating the array exceeds the block limit
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Users can no longer change their lock duration, and external contracts can no longer calculate weighted values

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent breakage of core protocol features
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires contract upgrade as there is no function to remove tokens from the array
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Lock duration updates and reward distribution

threats.title: setUSDThresholds Allows Zero or Extreme Values Breaking Governance
threats.hypothesis_description: setUSDThresholds has no bounds validation on _approve and _disapprove parameters. Setting DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD to 0 makes the condition disapprovalsCount < 0 impossible for uint8 (always false), preventing ANY proposal from executing. Setting APPROVE_THRESHOLD to 0 with high DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD allows proposals to execute with just the proposer's auto-approval since approvalsCount starts at 1.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _execUSDPriceUpdate condition requires both approvalsCount >= APPROVE_THRESHOLD AND disapprovalsCount < DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD. With DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD=0, the second condition is always false (uint8 disapprovalsCount >= 0 is always true), permanently blocking all price updates.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No bounds check on threshold values; _execUSDPriceUpdate: disapprovalsCount < DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD where DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD could be 0

preconditions: Admin calls setUSDThresholds with extreme values (0 or 255)
No proposal currently in progress (required by the function)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No minimum/maximum bounds on threshold values
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD=0 makes disapprovalsCount < 0 always false for uint8; all proposals permanently blocked

analysis_questions: Is admin a multisig or EOA?
Can admin fix by calling setUSDThresholds again?
Is there a timelock on admin actions?
validation_requirements: Set DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD to 0
Propose and attempt to approve a price update
Verify _execUSDPriceUpdate never executes

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Compromised or malicious admin sets DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD=0. Now _execUSDPriceUpdate requires disapprovalsCount < 0 which is impossible for uint8. All price updates are permanently blocked. USD prices become stale, distorting getLockedWeightedValue and downstream rewards.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent inability to update USD prices; stale prices distort reward distribution
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Admin can call setUSDThresholds again to fix, but requires recognizing the issue

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for extreme threshold values

threats.title: proposeUSDPrice Does Not Call _execUSDPriceUpdate When APPROVE_THRESHOLD Equals 1
threats.hypothesis_description: The proposeUSDPrice function auto-approves by setting approvalsCount=1 and the proposer's approval flag. However, it never calls _execUSDPriceUpdate. This function is only called inside approveUSDPrice. If APPROVE_THRESHOLD is set to 1, the proposer's auto-approval should be sufficient to execute, but no execution occurs. A second operator must call approveUSDPrice to trigger execution, effectively requiring minimum 2 approvals regardless of threshold.
threats.investigation_rationale: The semantic expectation of APPROVE_THRESHOLD=1 is that a single proposer can set the price. But the code requires at least one additional approveUSDPrice call. This is a functional correctness bug where the threshold semantics don't match implementation.
deployment_assumptions: Admin sets APPROVE_THRESHOLD to 1
threats.config_evidence: setUSDThresholds allows setting to 1; no bounds check prevents it

proof_of_signal.pattern: proposeUSDPrice sets approvalsCount++ but never checks if threshold is met or calls _execUSDPriceUpdate

preconditions: APPROVE_THRESHOLD set to 1 by admin
PriceFeed operator proposes a price

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Missing threshold check and _execUSDPriceUpdate call in proposeUSDPrice
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: approvalsCount=1 meets threshold=1 but execution never triggered
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should call _execUSDPriceUpdate at end of proposeUSDPrice if threshold met

analysis_questions: Is APPROVE_THRESHOLD=1 a realistic configuration?
What happens if the proposer tries to approve their own proposal?
Is there a workaround?
validation_requirements: Set APPROVE_THRESHOLD to 1
Call proposeUSDPrice
Verify proposal is NOT executed despite approvalsCount >= threshold
Verify proposer cannot call approveUSDPrice (ProposerCannotApproveError)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Admin sets APPROVE_THRESHOLD=1. PriceFeed operator proposes price. approvalsCount=1>=1 but _execUSDPriceUpdate not called. Proposer cannot approve (ProposerCannotApproveError). Another operator must approve, requiring 2 approvals despite threshold=1. Price updates become slower than intended.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Price updates delayed; stale prices affect reward calculations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users dependent on accurate USD pricing
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Another operator can approve; operational workaround exists

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible when proposal sits without execution

threats.title: ConfigStorage-driven dependency swapping can brick locking/unlocking if dependencies are set to non-contract or reverting addresses
threats.hypothesis_description: configUpdated() repoints critical dependencies (accountManager, migrationManager, nftOverlord, snuggeryManager) based solely on configStorage values. If configStorage is compromised or misconfigured, LockManager can be bricked (e.g., accountManager.forceHarvest or getPlayer reverts) or can grant unintended behavior (e.g., migrationManager set incorrectly changes NFT minting path).
threats.investigation_rationale: Not a pure code bug under normal trust assumptions, but a high-impact system wiring surface. Because LockManager has no sanity checks (code size, interface support), a bad update can halt user flows or change reward/NFT entitlement logic.
deployment_assumptions: ConfigStorage authority is compromised, misconfigured, or can be updated to incorrect addresses
No additional guards exist in BaseBlastManager/ConfigStorage to prevent invalid address updates
threats.config_evidence: Only scripts shown are generic deploy/config; no evidence of on-chain validation in LockManager for dependency addresses.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _reconfigure loads addresses from configStorage with no validation; lock/unlock depend on accountManager.forceHarvest/getPlayer

preconditions: configStorage updates addresses to invalid/reverting targets
onlyConfigStorage gate can be exercised (by intended authority or compromise)
Users call lock/unlock which invokes external dependency methods

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Mutable dependency injection without contract/interface sanity checks
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Protocol can be bricked or behavior changed by wrong dependency address
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No code.length checks; no try/catch; no version/interface validation

analysis_questions: Does ConfigStorage enforce code-hash allowlists or contract checks?
Is there an emergency rollback mechanism if a bad config update is made?
Are these dependencies upgradeable/proxy-based and can their behavior change unexpectedly?
validation_requirements: Review IConfigStorage and BaseBlastManager implementations for validation
Simulate configUpdated to EOA address; confirm lock/unlock revert on external calls
Assess operational procedures around config updates

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If config authority is compromised, attacker sets accountManager to a reverting contract, freezing all lock/unlock operations (funds stuck until config is repaired).

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Protocol-wide freeze of user funds (operational outage)
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Medium (depends on ability to restore correct config quickly)

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests for configUpdated sanity and dependency correctness checks

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: At time of config update and subsequent user actions
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: On-chain: sudden widespread reverts after configUpdated events

threats.title: setUSDThresholds Lacks Bounds Validation Enabling Governance Lockout or Bypass
threats.hypothesis_description: setUSDThresholds accepts arbitrary uint8 values for APPROVE_THRESHOLD and DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD without minimum/maximum validation. Setting DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD=0 makes `disapprovalsCount < 0` always false (uint8 underflow semantics in comparison), permanently blocking all price update executions. Setting APPROVE_THRESHOLD=0 with high DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD weakens the multisig to require only 1 approval (proposer's auto-approval, though limited by ECON-H009).
threats.investigation_rationale: An admin error or compromise could permanently brick the price governance system or weaken it to single-operator control, with cascading effects on reward distribution fairness.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setUSDThresholds assigns APPROVE_THRESHOLD = _approve and DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD = _disapprove without bounds checking

preconditions: Admin calls setUSDThresholds with extreme values (0, or > 5)
No active proposal at the time of the call

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD=0: in _execUSDPriceUpdate, `disapprovalsCount < 0` — uint8 comparison, 0 < 0 is false → no proposal can ever execute. APPROVE_THRESHOLD=255: impossible to reach with 5 operators → governance frozen.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Compromised admin sets DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD=0 → all future price updates blocked → prices become permanently stale.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker with admin access can permanently freeze price governance, then exploit stale prices for reward manipulation.

economic_questions: What threshold values render governance non-functional?
Can the admin fix broken thresholds (answer: yes, if no proposal is active)?
What is the recovery path after broken thresholds are set?
validation_requirements: Test _execUSDPriceUpdate with DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD=0
Test with APPROVE_THRESHOLD > 5 (unreachable with 5 operators)
Verify admin can reset thresholds when no proposal exists

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect — permanently stale prices enable reward manipulation
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on price deviation over time

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All protocol users relying on accurate price updates
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional to price divergence over frozen period

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test edge cases: 0, 1, 6, 255 for both thresholds

threats.title: Unbounded configuredTokenContracts Array Growth Enables Gas Exhaustion DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: The configuredTokenContracts array grows with each new token configured via configureToken (tokens are pushed but never removed). This array is iterated fully in setLockDuration, getLocked, and getLockedWeightedValue. With enough configured tokens, these functions exceed the block gas limit, permanently DoSing lock duration changes and reward calculations for all users.
threats.investigation_rationale: getLockedWeightedValue is likely called by external contracts for reward distribution. If it becomes uncallable due to gas limits, the entire reward system breaks. While admin-driven, the array has no bounds check and tokens cannot be removed once added.
threats.config_evidence: configure-token-test.ts adds 3 tokens. No limit visible in code. Admin could add more over time.

proof_of_signal.pattern: configuredTokenContracts.push(_tokenContract) with no upper bound; iterated in setLockDuration (line 241), getLocked (line 378), getLockedWeightedValue (line 400)

preconditions: Admin configures a large number of tokens (estimated 200+ for gas exhaustion)
Users attempt to call setLockDuration or external contracts call getLockedWeightedValue

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Each iteration: storage reads for lockedTokens and configuredTokens (~5000 gas per cold SLOAD). At 200 tokens: ~1M gas per iteration minimum.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Admin gradually adds tokens over protocol lifetime. At critical threshold, setLockDuration and getLockedWeightedValue become uncallable.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit — DoS of protocol functionality. Users cannot change lock durations; reward calculations break.

economic_questions: What is the realistic gas cost per configured token in each iterating function?
What is the Blast block gas limit?
How many tokens would realistically be configured over the protocol's lifetime?
validation_requirements: Benchmark gas costs of setLockDuration/getLockedWeightedValue with increasing token counts
Determine the threshold at which functions exceed block gas limit
Verify tokens cannot be removed from the array

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Cannot change lock duration; reward calculations may break

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Current token count: 3 (from deploy scripts); Blast gas limit ~30M

threats.title: setUSDThresholds Allows Zero or Extreme Values Breaking Price Governance
threats.hypothesis_description: setUSDThresholds has no bounds validation on _approve or _disapprove values. Admin can set DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD=0, which makes `disapprovalsCount < 0` always false for uint8 (impossible condition), permanently blocking ALL price update executions in _execUSDPriceUpdate. Conversely, setting APPROVE_THRESHOLD=0 allows unintended behavior since no approval count can be less than 0 for uint8.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _execUSDPriceUpdate condition requires `disapprovalsCount < DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD`. With DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD=0, this is `uint8 < 0` which is always false, making it impossible to execute any price update regardless of approval count. No recovery path exists within the contract since setUSDThresholds requires no active proposal.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No minimum/maximum bounds on `_approve` or `_disapprove` parameters; _execUSDPriceUpdate requires `disapprovalsCount < DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD` which is impossible when threshold=0

preconditions: Admin sets DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD to 0 (or other extreme value)
No active proposal at time of threshold change

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No bounds checking on uint8 threshold values
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent until admin corrects the threshold

operational_questions: What values have been historically set for thresholds?
Is there a multisig process protecting the admin key from misconfiguration?
Can the admin fix the issue by resetting thresholds (yes, if no active proposal)?
validation_requirements: Set DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD=0, propose and approve price update, verify execution is impossible

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: All subsequent price proposals cannot execute: `disapprovalsCount < 0` is always false
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: USD prices become permanently stale, distorting all weighted value calculations
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Admin must realize the error and reset threshold (requires no active proposal)

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Recoverable if admin is aware — set threshold back to valid value
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All USD price updates, weighted value calculations

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: None — simple function call with wrong parameter

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Price proposals never executing despite sufficient approvals

threats.title: Reconfiguration can brick locking/unlocking by setting critical dependency addresses to zero or incompatible contracts
threats.hypothesis_description: configUpdated() re-wires accountManager/migrationManager/snuggeryManager/nftOverlord from configStorage without validation. If configStorage returns address(0) or an incompatible contract, _lock and unlock can revert (e.g., accountManager.getPlayer/forceHarvest calls). This can freeze deposits/withdrawals and break NFT reveal accounting during lockdrop.
threats.investigation_rationale: While onlyConfigStorage gates configUpdated, the operational blast radius of a bad config push is large. This is a common production incident class (misconfiguration) and can be triggered by compromised configStorage governance as well.
deployment_assumptions: ConfigStorage can be updated post-deploy and may point to new addresses during upgrades/maintenance
No on-chain validation exists elsewhere to prevent setting zero or wrong-interface addresses
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence provided showing configStorage immutability or validation of StorageKey addresses; LockManager.configUpdated() performs unchecked assignments from configStorage.getAddress(...)

proof_of_signal.pattern: accountManager = IAccountManager(configStorage.getAddress(...)); nftOverlord = INFTOverlord(configStorage.getAddress(...)); no nonzero/code-size checks

preconditions: ConfigStorage (or its admin/governance) updates StorageKey.* addresses to zero or incorrect contract
Users attempt to lock/unlock (calls to accountManager/nftOverlord are executed)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Mutable wiring without runtime validation makes critical paths sensitive to config mistakes
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: High; correctness relies on configStorage integrity and update procedure
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During upgrades, migrations, emergency config changes

operational_questions: Who controls configStorage and what is the change management process?
Are there on-chain invariants enforced in configStorage (nonzero, contract codehash allowlist)?
Is there a rollback mechanism if configUpdated bricks locking?
validation_requirements: Inspect IConfigStorage implementation for access control and validation
Simulate setting AccountManager to address(0) and observe lock/unlock behavior
Check monitoring/alerts for config changes

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Push a config update in configStorage for one dependency
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call LockManager.configUpdated() (or rely on automated trigger)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: User operations revert due to bad external call targets

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Indirect control over protocol behavior via dependency substitution
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Protocol-wide deposit/withdraw halt; lockdrop NFT accounting failure
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium (depends on ability to correct config quickly)
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All user lock/unlock flows and lockdrop mint notifications

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: ConfigStorage updates followed by sudden revert spikes in lock/unlock

threats.title: ETH locking is inconsistent with receive() reverting, causing forced-ETH balance divergence and potential accounting confusion
threats.hypothesis_description: Contract explicitly supports ETH locks via lock/lockOnBehalf when _tokenContract==address(0), yet receive() and fallback() revert. ETH can still be forcibly sent (selfdestruct/coinbase), creating a contract ETH balance that is not attributable to any user's lockedTokens. This can confuse operational accounting and incident response (users claim missing funds; auditors see surplus ETH).
threats.investigation_rationale: This is operationally relevant on chains with selfdestruct semantics or forced ETH transfers, leading to real-world reconciliation issues. While not a direct theft vector, it complicates solvency monitoring and may mask shortfalls in other assets.
deployment_assumptions: The chain permits forced ETH transfers to contracts (e.g., via selfdestruct or protocol-level mechanisms)
Operational monitoring relies on contract.balance as a proxy for user-locked ETH
threats.config_evidence: LockManager has receive()/fallback() reverting but exposes ETH lock path; no explicit ETH reconciliation or sweep function shown

proof_of_signal.pattern: receive() external payable { revert LockManagerRefuseETHError(); } while _lock allows _tokenContract==address(0) with msg.value == _quantity

preconditions: Some ETH is forcibly sent to LockManager outside lock/lockOnBehalf
Operators/accounting systems interpret contract ETH balance as user-backed collateral

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: ETH is both refused and accepted depending on call path, indicating potential monitoring and integration mismatches
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time forced ETH transfers occur

operational_questions: Do monitoring dashboards reconcile user-locked ETH vs address(this).balance?
Is there a procedure to handle unsolicited ETH (sweep, donate to treasury, etc.)?
Can forced ETH be used to make unlock failures appear as if the contract is solvent?
validation_requirements: Test forced ETH transfer to contract and confirm no state reflects it
Review operational runbooks for balance reconciliation

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Force-send ETH to LockManager (e.g., selfdestruct from another contract)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Observe ETH balance increases without corresponding lockedTokens entries
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Exploit confusion in monitoring/incident response (masking deficits elsewhere)
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None (attacker can do on-chain forced send if chain permits)

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Incorrect solvency reporting; delayed detection of deficits; user disputes
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low to medium (requires process fixes and monitoring improvements)

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Incoming ETH transfers with no corresponding Locked events
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Contract ETH balance > sum of user-locked ETH quantities
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Small sporadic ETH dusting to LockManager

threats.title: Missing withdrawal/sweep path for stranded ERC20/ETH can create permanent asset accumulation and accounting drift
threats.hypothesis_description: LockManager holds ERC20s and potentially ETH (including forced ETH, positive rebases, airdrops). There is no function in this contract to sweep surplus tokens/ETH or recover tokens mistakenly sent directly. This can lead to permanent stranded funds, complicate migrations, and potentially block future upgrades if external assumptions rely on clean balances.
threats.investigation_rationale: Real protocols frequently receive airdrops/rebases/mistaken transfers. Without a sweep mechanism, balances diverge from user-accounted locked quantities, complicating solvency and migration. Not a direct theft, but an operational hazard.
deployment_assumptions: Tokens may rebase upward or airdrops may occur to LockManager
No other contract in the system has authority to move arbitrary ERC20s held by LockManager
threats.config_evidence: No sweep/rescue function visible in LockManager.sol; inheritance BaseBlastManager not shown (must be checked in Phase D)

proof_of_signal.pattern: fallback/receive revert; no other external function for arbitrary token/ETH recovery is present in this file

preconditions: LockManager receives assets outside normal lock flow (airdrop, positive rebase, mistaken ERC20 transfer, forced ETH)
No inherited/admin sweep function exists (to be validated)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Contract is an asset custodian but lacks explicit asset recovery tooling in this source unit
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Potentially none if no sweep exists anywhere; otherwise admin-run sweep procedures must be verified
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time external token mechanics send value to the contract

operational_questions: Does BaseBlastManager or any other manager provide a sweep/rescue mechanism?
Is the intended policy to keep all surplus in contract permanently?
How will migration handle surplus balances vs recorded locked quantities?
validation_requirements: Search inheritance tree for ERC20/ETH rescue functions and their access control
Simulate airdrop/rebase and confirm assets become stranded without a path

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Send an ERC20 directly to LockManager without calling lock()
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Observe no accounting entry is created but balance increases
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attempt to recover; fails if no sweep function exists

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Stranded value; accounting drift; migration complexity; potential inability to reconcile balances during incidents
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High if no sweep exists; otherwise depends on admin process

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: ERC20 Transfer events to LockManager without Locked events
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Airdrop campaigns targeting the LockManager address

threats.title: configUpdated Dependency Hot-Swap Enables Complete Fund Extraction via Malicious AccountManager
threats.hypothesis_description: The configUpdated() function, callable by configStorage, replaces all critical external contract references (accountManager, migrationManager, snuggeryManager, nftOverlord) in a single call without any timelock or validation. A compromised configStorage (or compromised admin who controls configStorage) can point accountManager to a malicious contract whose getPlayer() returns arbitrary data and whose forceHarvest() executes arbitrary logic. Since _lock calls accountManager.getPlayer and accountManager.forceHarvest BEFORE transferring tokens, and unlock calls forceHarvest before sending tokens, a malicious accountManager could manipulate state to enable unauthorized withdrawals or block legitimate ones.
threats.investigation_rationale: The entire trust model of LockManager depends on the integrity of external contract references loaded from configStorage. There is no timelock, no multi-sig requirement on configUpdated, and no validation of the new addresses. If configStorage governance is compromised, the attacker gains full control over all user funds in the LockManager. This represents the highest-impact single point of failure in the contract's dependency chain.
threats.config_evidence: configUpdated at LockManager.sol lines 81-83 has onlyConfigStorage guard but no timelock; _reconfigure replaces all four dependency addresses atomically

proof_of_signal.pattern: _reconfigure() replaces accountManager, migrationManager, snuggeryManager, nftOverlord from configStorage without any validation or timelock

preconditions: ConfigStorage contract is compromised or admin key controlling configStorage is compromised
Attacker deploys malicious replacement contracts (e.g., fake AccountManager)
Attacker calls configStorage to update addresses and trigger configUpdated on LockManager

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No validation on replacement addresses in _reconfigure(); configStorage.getAddress returns attacker-controlled address
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Malicious accountManager.getPlayer could allow any address to pass main-account checks; malicious forceHarvest could call back or manipulate state. Malicious migrationManager address would bypass NFT minting restrictions in _lock.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Total TVL of LockManager at risk; attacker can manipulate unlock flows or inject new lock entries

economic_questions: What governance model protects configStorage from unauthorized changes?
Is configStorage itself upgradeable or has its own admin key?
What is the total TVL held by LockManager?
validation_requirements: Map the full configStorage trust chain and access controls
Verify whether configStorage has timelock or multi-sig governance
Test deployment of LockManager with malicious configStorage to confirm full control over dependency references

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Gas for deploying malicious contracts and triggering configUpdated (~0.01 ETH)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to 100% of all locked assets in the contract
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Single block once configStorage is compromised

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: None; direct fund extraction from contract
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Total loss of user trust; protocol-ending event

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: configStorage access control model; who can call configStorage to trigger configUpdated
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify _reconfigure accepts any address from configStorage without validation

threats.title: Admin misconfiguration of configuredTokens.decimals > 18 can revert getLockedWeightedValue(), disrupting reward accounting that depends on it
threats.hypothesis_description: getLockedWeightedValue computes `10 ** (18 - decimals)`. If any configured token has decimals > 18, the subtraction underflows and reverts, potentially breaking downstream reward distribution or other protocol operations that require this call.
threats.investigation_rationale: While primarily configuration-driven, it can become a production outage if rewards contracts rely on getLockedWeightedValue and there is no fallback. This is especially relevant given docs expect support for very high decimals.
deployment_assumptions: Admin configures at least one token with decimals > 18
A critical state-changing path in another contract calls getLockedWeightedValue (or uses it as a prerequisite) such that the revert blocks operations
threats.config_evidence: Test script configures decimals=18; no evidence for production configs or any downstream caller dependencies.

proof_of_signal.pattern: deltaDecimal = 10 ** (18 - configuredTokens[token].decimals); underflow if decimals>18 (Solidity 0.8.x reverts)

preconditions: At least one configured token has decimals > 18
Some operational workflow depends on getLockedWeightedValue not reverting

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: A single bad token config can break weighted-value computations for all users (loop reverts).

operational_questions: Is getLockedWeightedValue used by any state-changing reward distribution function elsewhere?
Is there a validation step on configureToken to bound decimals to <=18?
validation_requirements: Static scan: find all call sites to getLockedWeightedValue in the system
Config test: configure decimals=19 and confirm revert; assess impact on dependent operations

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Any dependent workflow calling getLockedWeightedValue begins reverting.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Potential protocol-wide outage if weighted values are required for rewards/eligibility.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low to medium—can be fixed by re-configuring decimals, but may require governance process.
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Reward computations and any component relying on weighted locked value

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: TokenConfigured events setting decimals>18; spikes in reverts from getLockedWeightedValue
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Single config change correlated with outage
